mappings.rationaleAI: Stoicism from Zeno through Chrysippus, Epictetus, Seneca, and Marcus Aurelius directly covers the philosophy of the Stoics (A.1.c.i).
Epicureanism from Epicurus through Lucretius covers the teaching of Epicurus concerning the universe, pleasure, and man's relationship to gods (A.1.c.ii).
Scepticism from Pyrrho through Sextus Empiricus covers the teaching of Pyrrhon concerning man's lack of certainty (A.1.c.iii) and the preservation of ancient doctrines.
Neoplatonism from Plotinus through Porphyry and Proclus covers the teaching of Plotinus concerning levels of being (A.1.c.iv) and Neo-Pythagorean schools.
Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology directly covers the age of the Schoolmen (A.2.c).
Averroes' commentaries on Aristotle and their influence on medieval scholasticism address the contribution of Arabic philosophy (A.2.b).
Avicenna's synthesis of Aristotelian and Neoplatonic thought addresses the role of Islamic philosophers in increasing Aristotle's influence in the West (A.2.b).
Al-Kindi's role in the translation movement and reconciliation of Greek philosophy with Islamic theology connects to Arabic philosophy's contribution (A.2.b).
Empiricism from Locke through Hume covers developments in the Empiricist tradition (A.3.b.i) and Locke and Berkeley's inquiry into knowledge (A.3.c.i).
Spinoza's pantheistic Ethics and rationalist metaphysics cover developments in the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b.ii).
Hobbes's Leviathan and materialist philosophy cover political theory in the Renaissance (A.3.a.i) and developments in the Empiricist tradition (A.3.b.i).
Materialism from ancient atomism through dialectical materialism covers Epicurean atomism (A.1.c.ii), Rationalist developments (A.3.b.ii via Hobbes), and dialectical Materialism of Marx (A.3.d.ii).
Hegel's philosophy of history and dialectical method address the Idealism of Hegel (A.3.d.i).
Existentialism from Kierkegaard and Nietzsche through Heidegger and Sartre covers the reaction against Rationalism (A.3.d.iii) and independent philosophies (A.3.e.i).
Pragmatism from Peirce, James, and Dewey covers William James's and Dewey's Pragmatism (A.3.e.i).
Hume's radical empiricism, problem of induction, and critique of causation cover Hume's science of man (A.3.c.i).
Virtue from Socrates through Aristotle covers Socrates's ethical concerns (A.1.b.i) and Aristotle's ethics (A.1.b.iii).
The soul from Plato through Descartes covers Platonic metaphysics (A.1.b.ii), Neoplatonist thought on levels of being (A.1.c.iv), and Cartesian dualism (A.3.b.ii).
Plato's Republic covering the Theory of Forms, the philosopher-king, and epistemology addresses the philosophy of Plato (A.1.b.ii).
Aristotle's Politics addresses his empirical researches on political institutions (A.1.b.iii).
Zen Buddhism covers Chan Buddhism's synthesis of Buddhist and Daoist elements, relevant to Japanese philosophy (B.3).
The Han Synthesis blending Confucian, Legalist, and Daoist thought addresses Chinese philosophy including Neo-Confucianism (B.2.c).
Cicero's philosophical works transmitting Stoicism and Skepticism to Rome address both Stoic (A.1.c.i) and Skeptic (A.1.c.iii) philosophy in the Hellenistic-Roman period.
Bacon's attempt to formulate a new scientific method through empirical induction addresses developments in the Empiricist tradition (A.3.b.i).
Deism's development of natural religion and rejection of revelation addresses developments in religious philosophy during the Enlightenment (A.3.c.iii).
Romanticism's reaction against Enlightenment rationalism connects to the reaction against Rationalism including Schopenhauer and Kierkegaard (A.3.d.iii).
The concept of freedom from Hobbes through Rousseau and Mill addresses the concern of Locke and Rousseau with freedom and equality (A.3.c.iii).
Alcuin's role in preserving classical learning connects to philosophy and the liberal arts in Christian West schools (A.2.a.ii).
Friendship from Aristotle's Nicomachean Ethics through Epicurean friendship addresses Aristotle's ethics (A.1.b.iii) and Epicurean philosophy (A.1.c.ii).
